original_abstract:
Modern scientific experiments increasingly rely on the simultaneous control and monitoring of multiple asynchronous data streams. Integrating these heterogeneous sources—ranging from high-speed video to neural telemetry—poses a significant challenge for researchers, as traditional sequential programming models struggle to handle parallel execution and complex hardware integration efficiently. Here, we present Bonsai, an open-source visual programming framework designed for the rapid prototyping of data acquisition and processing pipelines. 

Bonsai is built upon a reactive, event-driven architecture that represents data streams as observable sequences. This modular framework allows users to compose complex workflows using a dataflow representation, incorporating advanced operators for time-slicing, windowing, and the implementation of nested state machines. We demonstrate the versatility and performance of Bonsai through several diverse applications in neuroscience, including real-time animal tracking, high-channel-count electrophysiology, multi-modal behavioral assays, and closed-loop optogenetic stimulation. By providing a high-performance environment that bridges the gap between specialized hardware and flexible software control, Bonsai empowers researchers to implement sophisticated experimental designs with minimal computational overhead.
humanized_abstract:
In modern scientific research it is becoming increasingly common that many experiments require real-time control and monitoring of numerous asynchronous signal streams. Sources such as video streams, live physiological neural activity from animals (brain wave data), remote sensors, strain gauges, among many others, need to be integrated, controlled and analyzed in near real time and are a major obstacle in experimentation due to the lack of ease of use of sequential code in handling multichannel, simultaneous source processing and the increased complexity of managing the hardware interfaces. We present Bonsai, a visual workflow system for data acquisition and processing which enables rapid experimentation and allows researchers to construct data processing workflows much quicker and with less code.

Bonsai is a reactive, event-driven system based on observable sequences to represent data streams. The system enables composition of dataflow workflows from basic building blocks and high-level processing operations such as time-sliced processing, window functions and nested state machines. We show the applicability and performance of Bonsai with a variety of applications in neuroscience, ranging from real-time animal tracking, high-channel-count electrophysiology and multi-modal behavioral assays to closed-loop optogenetic stimulation. Bonsai forms a high-performance environment bridging the gap between hardware specialization and software flexibility that allows researchers to implement sophisticated experimental designs in an efficient way.
